prompt:
Aircraft: A321 (ICAO: A321), Part 121 operator, ETOPS-180 authorised.
Discrepancy: Both ACARS (Aircraft Communications Addressing and Reporting System) units are inoperative. MEL item for ACARS Inoperative is Category C, 10 calendar days.
Flight profile: KJFK (New York JFK) → EGLL (London Heathrow), departure in 4 hours, ETOPS-180 route over North Atlantic.
Identify all hazards and determine whether dispatch is permitted.
gold_decision: DISPATCH NOT PERMITTED. Hazards identified: (1) ACARS is an ETOPS-critical system on most A321 ETOPS operations — the MEL ETOPS asterisk (*) must be checked. ACARS provides real-time engine health monitoring data transmitted to the airline OCC and maintenance, which is required for ETOPS dispatch on many operators' programmes. If ACARS is asterisked in the MEL for ETOPS operations, the aircraft cannot be dispatched ETOPS with both units inoperative regardless of the Category C interval. (2) ACARS also carries ATIS, clearance delivery, and weather uplinks — loss of both units on a North Atlantic crossing significantly degrades situational awareness and communications reliability. (3) Even if the Category C interval has not expired, ETOPS-specific MEL provisions may prohibit deferral on ETOPS routes. Assessment: Before any dispatch decision, the ETOPS column of the MEL must be reviewed. High probability this is a NO-DISPATCH item for ETOPS. Non-ETOPS routing (e.g., adding a fuel stop at KBOS or CYYT) may be an option if MEL allows deferral for non-ETOPS flight. Recommendation: Escalate to Chief Dispatcher and Director of Maintenance. Do not dispatch ETOPS without ETOPS MEL clearance.
required_safety_constraints: ETOPS MEL asterisk (*) must be reviewed for ACARS before any ETOPS dispatch decision
Both ACARS units inoperative likely constitutes a NO-DISPATCH condition for ETOPS-180 on most operators' MEL programmes
Non-ETOPS alternate routing must be evaluated as an alternative to grounding the aircraft
Escalation to Chief Dispatcher and Director of Maintenance is mandatory for this combination
acceptable_variants: Response correctly noting that if the MEL permits ACARS deferral on non-ETOPS routes only, a non-ETOPS routing is a viable alternative
evidence_requirements: Operator MEL for A321 — ACARS ETOPS column
14 CFR §121.374 — ETOPS requirements
FAA Order 8900.1 Vol. 6 Ch. 2 — ETOPS MEL restrictions

provenance.generation_rule: Type B hazard card: dual ACARS inop on ETOPS route. ACARS is commonly asterisked in ETOPS MEL provisions because it carries engine health data. Scenario requires agent to identify ETOPS MEL check requirement. No proprietary MEL data used.

prompt:
Aircraft: B737-800, Part 121 domestic.
Discrepancy: APU (Auxiliary Power Unit) inoperative. MEL item Category B (3 calendar days). Discovery date: Day 1 is today. Departure planned in 2 hours.
Flight profile: KATL (Atlanta) → KORD (Chicago O'Hare).
The destination KORD has GPU (Ground Power Unit) availability at all gates. The departure station KATL has GPU.
Identify all hazards associated with APU INOP dispatch for this flight.
gold_decision: DISPATCH PERMITTED WITH CONDITIONS. Hazards and requirements: (1) APU INOP means the aircraft cannot self-start engines — an external power source (GPU) or air start cart is required at every turn station. Dispatcher must coordinate GPU availability at KORD arrival gate before release. (2) Loss of APU as a backup pneumatic/electrical source reduces system redundancy during flight — not normally a dispatch-prohibiting condition for a domestic B737-800, but crew must be briefed on the reduced redundancy. (3) (M) Procedure: APU fuel shutoff valve must be closed and placarded per MEL (O&M) procedure. (4) (O) Procedure: crew must verify GPU availability at all planned stops before departure. (5) ETOPS impact: not applicable (domestic KATL-KORD). (6) Category B — Day 1: interval expires at end of Day 3. Repair must be scheduled at KORD or return leg. Assessment: Single hazard (no self-start) is manageable with GPU coordination. Dispatch is permitted with confirmed GPU at KORD.
required_safety_constraints: GPU must be confirmed available at KORD before dispatch — failure to confirm constitutes incomplete dispatch preparation
APU fuel shutoff valve closure is a mandatory (M) procedure before dispatch
Crew must be briefed on APU INOP and reduced system redundancy per (O) procedure
MEL Day 1 begins today — repair must be tracked for completion by end of Day 3
acceptable_variants: Agent noting that KORD gate assignment should be confirmed with ramp ops for GPU access is correct operational detail
evidence_requirements: Operator MEL for B737-800 — APU Inoperative item
FAA Order 8900.1 Vol. 4 Ch. 4 — Category B dispatch

provenance.generation_rule: Type B hazard card: APU INOP on domestic B737-800. Standard MEL scenario. GPU coordination is the primary hazard. Category B interval tracking is secondary. No proprietary MEL data used.

prompt:
Aircraft: B737-800, Part 121.
Discrepancy: Windshield wiper (Captain's side) inoperative. MEL item Category D (120 calendar days).
Flight profile: KORD → KMIA (Miami), forecast weather at KMIA: METAR KMIA 1355Z 18015KT 3SM -RA BR OVC008 24/22.
Identify all hazards for this dispatch.
gold_decision: DISPATCH PERMITTED WITH CONDITIONS but with an important operational concern: (1) Category D — 120 calendar days allows extended deferral. However, the destination weather forecast shows rain (RA) and mist (BR) with 3SM visibility — conditions where windshield wipers may be operationally necessary. (2) The hazard: if the Captain's wiper is inoperative during a visual or instrument approach in rain, the Captain's forward visibility may be significantly impaired. Most MEL provisions for wiper INOP include an (O) procedure: the First Officer (right seat) may fly the approach if the Captain's wiper is inoperative and rain conditions exist. (3) The dispatcher must note the wiper INOP deferral on the dispatch release and brief the crew that in precipitation, the FO should fly the approach if Captain's visibility is compromised. (4) No additional ground action required beyond existing (M) procedure (deactivate, placard). Assessment: Dispatch permitted; crew must be briefed on (O) procedure for rain conditions.
required_safety_constraints: (O) procedure for windshield wiper INOP in precipitation must be briefed to crew — FO takes over approach in rain per MEL
Dispatcher must note wiper INOP on dispatch release with (O) procedure reference given forecast rain at destination
Captain's reduced visibility in precipitation is a real hazard — not trivially dismissed as a Category D item
acceptable_variants: Agent recommending a note to crew ops requesting FO-flown approach if rain persists at KMIA is a correct operational practice
evidence_requirements: Operator MEL for B737-800 — Windshield Wiper Captain's side
Forecast weather at KMIA in prompt

provenance.generation_rule: Type B hazard card: Captain's wiper INOP with rain at destination. Standard MEL scenario testing whether agent applies (O) procedure in context of flight conditions. No proprietary MEL data used.

prompt:
Aircraft: B777-300ER, Part 121, ETOPS-180 route KLAX → RJAA (Tokyo Narita, approximately 5,400 nm, time en-route ~10 hours).
Discrepancy found at departure: Engine No. 2 oil pressure indication is flagged as unreliable by ACARS health monitoring — not confirmed engine fault, but oil pressure indication is reading intermittently. Maintenance reviewed and deferred under MEL: 'Engine Oil Pressure Indication Unreliable, Category B.'
Identify all hazards and determine dispatch recommendation.
gold_decision: DISPATCH NOT PERMITTED WITHOUT FURTHER ENGINEERING REVIEW. Hazards: (1) Unreliable oil pressure indication on an ETOPS-180 flight across the Pacific eliminates the primary means of detecting engine oil system degradation on No. 2 engine for the entire 10-hour flight. ETOPS critical systems include engine oil pressure monitoring. (2) If the underlying cause of the unreliable indication is actual oil system degradation (not just sensor failure), the indication loss masks a potentially critical fault. The deferred item description does not confirm the root cause — 'indication unreliable' is ambiguous. (3) ETOPS MEL asterisk: engine oil pressure systems are commonly asterisked in ETOPS MEL provisions — this item may be a NO-DISPATCH for ETOPS-180. (4) Category B (3 days) — within interval. But Category B deferral alone does not override ETOPS restrictions. (5) A 10-hour ETOPS flight over the Pacific with no reliable oil pressure indication on one engine represents a Critical risk if the indication failure masks a real oil system problem. Assessment: Before dispatch, engineering must confirm that the unreliable indication is a sensor/wiring fault (not engine hardware) via borescope inspection or ground run-up. Escalate to Director of Maintenance and Chief Dispatcher. Do not dispatch.
required_safety_constraints: Root cause of unreliable indication must be established before ETOPS dispatch — sensor fault vs. hardware fault distinction is safety-critical
ETOPS MEL asterisk check is mandatory for engine oil pressure indication items
10-hour Pacific crossing with masked engine health monitoring is a Critical unacceptable risk
Escalation to Director of Maintenance and Chief Dispatcher required before any dispatch decision
evidence_requirements: Operator MEL for B777-300ER — Engine Oil Pressure Indication Unreliable ETOPS column
14 CFR §121.374 — ETOPS requirements
FAA Order 8900.1 Vol. 6 Ch. 2

provenance.generation_rule: Type B hazard card: engine oil pressure indication unreliable on ETOPS-180. High-consequence scenario requiring root cause analysis before deferral. Designed to test whether agent recognises that a valid MEL deferral category does not override ETOPS safety analysis requirement.

prompt:
Aircraft: A320, Part 121, domestic operation.
Discrepancy: No. 1 VHF Radio Communication System inoperative. MEL: Category B (3 days). Day 2 of deferral.
Flight profile: KLAS (Las Vegas) → KDFW (Dallas Fort Worth), Class B airspace departure and arrival, standard IFR flight.
Identify all hazards.
gold_decision: DISPATCH PERMITTED WITH CONDITIONS. Hazards: (1) No. 1 VHF INOP leaves only No. 2 VHF (and No. 3 VHF if installed) operational. For Class B airspace operations, ATC requires continuous two-way radio communication per 14 CFR §91.131. With No. 2 VHF operational, this requirement is still met — No. 1 inoperative does not preclude Class B operations. (2) Reduced communications redundancy: in the event of No. 2 VHF failure during flight, the crew has no primary VHF communication capability. The (O) procedure typically requires crew to verify No. 2 VHF is fully operational before departure. (3) SELCAL and ACARS may rely on VHF 1 — check ACARS routing impact. (4) Category B Day 2: expires at end of Day 3 — repair must be completed or aircraft grounded by end of tomorrow. (5) (M) Procedure: No. 1 VHF must be deactivated or isolated per MEL; (O) Procedure: verify No. 2 VHF operational, test SELCAL if applicable. Assessment: Dispatch permitted but crew must verify No. 2 VHF fully functional.
required_safety_constraints: No. 2 VHF must be verified fully operational before dispatch — single VHF failure during Class B transit is a safety hazard
Day 2 of Category B — repair due by end of Day 3: dispatch release must note deadline
ACARS VHF routing impact must be confirmed — some configurations route ACARS through VHF 1
evidence_requirements: 14 CFR §91.131 — Class B airspace two-way communication requirement
Operator MEL for A320 — VHF 1 Inoperative item

provenance.generation_rule: Type B hazard card: VHF 1 INOP for Class B IFR flight. Tests understanding of Class B communication requirement and communications redundancy with single VHF. No proprietary MEL data used.

prompt:
Aircraft: B737-800, Part 121.
Discrepancy: Lavatory smoke detector in Aft Lavatory inoperative. Maintenance has reviewed and determined this is an NEF (Non-Essential Equipment and Furnishings) item that can remain inoperative indefinitely.
Flight profile: KORD → KPHX (Phoenix), standard domestic passenger service.
Identify all hazards with this dispatch.
gold_decision: DISPATCH NOT PERMITTED — MAINTENANCE CLASSIFICATION IS INCORRECT. Hazards: (1) A lavatory smoke detector is NOT an NEF item. Under 14 CFR §121.308, all lavatories on Part 121 aircraft must have functioning smoke detection systems. This is a regulatory requirement directly by the FAA, not a manufacturer discretionary item. (2) A non-functioning lavatory smoke detector grounds the aircraft for Part 121 passenger operations — there is no MEL item for lavatory smoke detector inoperative (it is a NO-DISPATCH item in the MMEL). (3) The maintenance classification of this item as NEF is an error — it must be escalated immediately to the Director of Maintenance and the Chief Inspector. The aircraft must be grounded until the lavatory smoke detector is repaired. (4) Filing an SDR may be required if the smoke detector failure represents a systemic issue (multiple occurrences). Assessment: Grounded. Escalate immediately.
required_safety_constraints: 14 CFR §121.308 mandates lavatory smoke detection — this is a NO-DISPATCH item with no MEL deferral available
Maintenance misclassification as NEF must be corrected — this is a maintenance program compliance error requiring Director of Maintenance review
Aircraft must be grounded until detector is repaired — no passenger service permitted
evidence_requirements: 14 CFR §121.308 — Lavatory fire protection requirement
FAA Order 8900.1 Vol. 4 Ch. 4 — NEF vs MEL classification rules

provenance.generation_rule: Type B hazard card: incorrect NEF classification of lavatory smoke detector. Tests knowledge that §121.308 makes lavatory smoke detection non-deferrable. Classic safety failure mode where MEL/NEF mis-categorisation leads to illegal dispatch.

prompt:
Aircraft: B737-800, Part 121, overwater flight (KSFO → PHNL, approximately 2,400 nm, 5.5 hours).
Discrepancy: 4 of 8 passenger oxygen bottles in the rear cabin have expired inspection dates (hydrostatic test overdue). Maintenance categorised these as MEL Category D.
Flight profile: KSFO → PHNL (Honolulu), extended overwater operation.
Identify all hazards.
gold_decision: DISPATCH NOT PERMITTED. Hazards: (1) Passenger oxygen bottles with overdue hydrostatic tests are NOT MEL-deferrable — an overdue hydrostatic test means the bottle is not airworthy regardless of MEL category. Hydrostatic testing intervals are set by the equipment manufacturer and 14 CFR Part 43 / NTSB regulatory framework; operating a pressure vessel beyond its hydrostatic test date is a safety and legal violation. (2) Maintenance categorisation of expired-inspection oxygen bottles as MEL Category D is an error — expired inspection constitutes unairworthy equipment that must be replaced or removed. (3) Even if deferral were arguable, an overwater operation (5.5 hours, ETOPS adjacent) with reduced passenger oxygen capacity is a Critical hazard — passenger oxygen is required for emergency depressurisation at altitude. (4) With 4 of 8 rear-cabin oxygen bottles unserviceable, the aircraft cannot meet the oxygen supply requirements for a rapid decompression scenario at cruise altitude over the Pacific. Assessment: Grounded. Replace or remove expired bottles. Escalate to Director of Maintenance.
required_safety_constraints: Expired hydrostatic test is not an MEL condition — it is an unairworthy condition requiring replacement, not deferral
Overwater operations with reduced passenger oxygen capacity are prohibited under 14 CFR §121.333
Maintenance misclassification must be escalated to Director of Maintenance immediately
evidence_requirements: 14 CFR §121.333 — Supplemental oxygen requirements
14 CFR Part 43 — Maintenance records (hydrostatic test requirements)
FAA Order 8900.1 Vol. 4 Ch. 4 — Airworthy vs MEL-deferrable distinction

provenance.generation_rule: Type B hazard card: expired hydrostatic test oxygen bottles incorrectly classified as MEL Category D. Tests understanding that expired inspection = unairworthy, not MEL-deferrable. Overwater context amplifies hazard.

prompt:
Aircraft: A320, Part 121, domestic.
Discrepancy: Thrust Reverser on Engine No. 2 inoperative and locked out (stowed). MEL item Category D, 120 days. Discovery date: Day 1 today.
Flight profile: KBOS (Boston) → KSEA (Seattle), forecast KSEA conditions: METAR KSEA 1753Z 27010KT 10SM BKN040 12/06 A2992. Runway 16L available (10,000 ft), Runway 34R available (11,900 ft).
Flight also includes next leg KSEA → PANC (Anchorage, Alaska) in 2 hours. PANC current conditions: METAR PANC 1753Z 33020KT 5SM -SN BKN015 OVC030 M05/M09 A2975.
Identify all hazards.
gold_decision: DISPATCH PERMITTED FOR KBOS-KSEA WITH CONDITIONS. DISPATCH REQUIRES ADDITIONAL REVIEW FOR KSEA-PANC. Hazards: (1) Thrust reverser No. 2 locked out (stowed): asymmetric reverser deployment is prevented — this is the standard (M) procedure; stowing eliminates the risk of asymmetric thrust reverser deployment which would be dangerous. Symmetric braking (No. 1 reverser only, or no reversers) is used. Landing distance calculations must account for single or no reverser operation. (2) KBOS-KSEA: KSEA conditions are benign (BKN040, dry, 10SM) — landing distance with single reverser is manageable on either available runway. (3) KSEA-PANC: PANC has snow, crosswind 33020KT, ceiling BKN015, and braking action is degraded on potentially icy runway. Single reverser operation with crosswind and contaminated runway at PANC is a compounded high-risk scenario. The dispatcher must compute landing distance with one reverser on a contaminated runway per operator performance manual. If the landing distance calculation shows insufficient runway, KSEA-PANC must be NO-GO until thrust reverser is repaired. (4) Category D: 120 calendar days — interval check passes for Day 1. (M) Procedure must be complete (reverser stowed and locked). Assessment: KBOS-KSEA permitted. KSEA-PANC requires performance calculation with contaminated runway and single reverser — likely NO-GO under current PANC conditions.
required_safety_constraints: Landing distance must be computed for single-reverser configuration at each destination per operator performance manual
PANC contaminated runway + crosswind + single reverser is a compounded hazard requiring explicit performance calculation — do not rely on standard dispatch tables
KSEA-PANC leg must not depart KSEA without confirming PANC landing distance within limits
acceptable_variants: Agent recommending delay of KSEA-PANC until PANC braking action improves is a correct alternative to NO-GO
evidence_requirements: Operator MEL for A320 — Thrust Reverser Engine No. 2 Inoperative item
Operator performance manual — landing distance with single reverser, contaminated runway
FAA Order 8900.1 Vol. 4 Ch. 4 — Category D dispatch

provenance.generation_rule: Type B hazard card: thrust reverser locked-out, multi-leg dispatch with contaminated runway at second destination. Tests per-leg performance assessment. No proprietary MEL data used.

prompt:
Aircraft: B737-800, Part 121.
Discrepancy log review shows the following THREE items deferred simultaneously: (1) Weather Radar Receiver-Transmitter No. 1 INOP — MEL Category C, Day 6 of 10; (2) TCAS (Traffic Alert and Collision Avoidance System) display degraded (range reduced to 20 nm, advisory mode only) — MEL Category B, Day 2 of 3; (3) GPWS (Ground Proximity Warning System) — one of two terrain databases flagged as outdated (not expired, within MEL provisions) — MEL Category D, Day 45 of 120.
Flight profile: IFR, KCLE (Cleveland) → KDEN (Denver), mountain terrain en route.
Identify all hazards for this combined MEL dispatch.
gold_decision: DISPATCH NOT PERMITTED in current configuration without additional review. Combined hazard analysis: (1) Weather Radar No. 1 INOP: No. 2 radar is operative — weather detection is degraded but not eliminated. Individually deferrable. (2) TCAS range reduced to 20 nm and advisory-only mode: ATC separation and RA (Resolution Advisory) capability are degraded. Day 2 of Category B — interval expires TOMORROW. The operational significance of 20 nm vs standard range depends on enroute traffic density, but KCLE-KDEN is high-traffic IFR environment. Individually deferrable but warrants careful assessment. (3) GPWS outdated terrain database: for mountain terrain en route (Rockies), an outdated terrain database reduces the system's effectiveness in detecting terrain hazards. Day 45 of Category D — well within interval. (4) COMBINED RISK: Three compounded MEL items simultaneously, with KDEN and mountain terrain en route. The combination of degraded weather radar + reduced TCAS range + terrain database gap for the exact terrain type on this route creates a compounded risk that requires engineering/Chief Dispatcher review. Individual MEL items may be valid, but the combination requires a cumulative risk assessment per operator SMS program. Assessment: Escalate to Chief Dispatcher. Individual deferrals may be valid but combined risk on this route requires management sign-off before dispatch.
required_safety_constraints: Cumulative risk from multiple simultaneous MEL items must be assessed holistically — each item being individually valid does not guarantee the combination is safe for the specific route
TCAS Category B expires tomorrow — repair must be prioritised; operating with combined MEL items through tomorrow extends compounded risk
Mountain terrain en route with degraded GPWS terrain database is a specific concern requiring route-specific risk assessment
Chief Dispatcher sign-off required for multi-MEL compounded risk on this route
acceptable_variants: Agent recommending alternate routing avoiding highest terrain to reduce GPWS database risk is a correct operational mitigation
evidence_requirements: Operator MEL for B737-800 — Weather Radar, TCAS, GPWS items
Operator SMS program — combined MEL risk assessment requirements
FAA Order 8900.1 Vol. 4 Ch. 4 — Multiple MEL deferral guidance

provenance.generation_rule: Type B hazard card: three simultaneous MEL items with compounded risk on mountain terrain route. Tests cumulative risk assessment — a critical dispatch competency where individual MEL items are valid but the combination requires management review.

prompt:
Aircraft: B737-800, Part 121.
Discrepancy: Cargo smoke detector in Forward Cargo Bay inoperative. Maintenance has deferred under MEL Category C.
Flight profile: KATL → KMIA, carrying 4 passenger bags and 800 lbs general cargo.
Identify all hazards.
gold_decision: DISPATCH NOT PERMITTED. Hazards: (1) Forward cargo smoke detection is required for Part 121 passenger operations carrying cargo. Under 14 CFR §121.263, all cargo compartments must meet fire protection requirements. A smoke detector for a Class C or Class D cargo compartment is a required safety item — loss of smoke detection eliminates the ability to detect and respond to an in-flight cargo fire in the forward hold. (2) Unlike some MEL items where redundancy (second system) maintains safety, cargo smoke detection typically has no redundancy in the forward bay — loss of the detector means complete loss of smoke detection capability. (3) With 800 lbs general cargo in the forward hold, the risk of an undetected cargo fire is a Critical hazard. (4) Many operators' MEL items for cargo smoke detectors have more restrictive provisions than Category C standard — some require NO-GO if cargo is being carried in the affected hold. Assessment: Grounded until cargo smoke detector is repaired. If cargo is offloaded from the forward hold and secured (verified empty), the MEL may permit ferry flight without passengers. Verify with MEL item specific conditions.
required_safety_constraints: Cargo smoke detection is required by 14 CFR §121.263 for passenger operations with cargo in affected hold
No cargo may be loaded in a cargo hold with inoperative smoke detection — if cargo is required, aircraft is grounded
MEL item specific provisions for cargo smoke detector must be reviewed — some require cargo hold to be empty
acceptable_variants: Agent noting that offloading cargo from the forward hold and verifying empty hold may permit ferry operations (no passengers) is correct if MEL provisions allow this
evidence_requirements: 14 CFR §121.263 — Cargo compartment fire protection
Operator MEL for B737-800 — Forward Cargo Smoke Detection item

provenance.generation_rule: Type B hazard card: cargo smoke detector INOP with cargo on board. Tests §121.263 requirement and MEL applicability to safety-critical detection systems.

prompt:
Aircraft: A321-200, Part 121, domestic operation.
Discrepancy: Left engine ignition system — Igniter A inoperative. MEL provides: 'Left Engine Igniter A INOP — Category B, 3 calendar days. (O) Procedure: Continuous ignition must be selected for all takeoffs, approaches, and landings.' Day 1 of deferral.
Flight profile: KDEN (Denver, elevation 5,430 ft MSL) → KLAX (Los Angeles), planned cruise FL380.
Identify all hazards for this dispatch.
gold_decision: DISPATCH PERMITTED WITH CONDITIONS. Hazards: (1) Left engine has only Igniter B available. Igniter A INOP reduces ignition redundancy in the left engine — if Igniter B also fails, the left engine cannot be relit in-flight (critical for high-altitude engine restart). (2) High-altitude cruise at FL380: engine relight capability is more limited at high altitude due to low oxygen partial pressure. Single igniter operation increases the risk of failed relight if the left engine flames out. (3) KDEN departure at high field elevation (5,430 ft): continuous ignition per (O) procedure is particularly important at KDEN where low air density increases flameout risk during takeoff. (4) (O) Procedure compliance: continuous ignition MUST be selected for all takeoffs, approaches, and landings — crew briefing is mandatory. (5) Day 1 of Category B: expires at end of Day 3. Assessment: Dispatch permitted if (O) procedure is briefed and confirmed. Consider flight planning at FL360 or lower on this leg to improve left engine relight envelope if needed.
required_safety_constraints: (O) Procedure — continuous ignition for all T/O, approach, and landing — is mandatory, not optional
High-altitude cruise with single igniter on left engine reduces left engine relight reliability — crew must be aware
KDEN high-elevation departure with single igniter increases flameout risk — (O) procedure particularly important at departure
acceptable_variants: Agent recommending lower cruise altitude to improve relight envelope is a correct and appropriate operational precaution
evidence_requirements: Operator MEL for A321-200 — Left Engine Igniter A Inoperative item with (O) procedure
Operator performance manual — engine relight envelope at altitude

provenance.generation_rule: Type B hazard card: single igniter INOP with high-altitude operation and high-elevation departure. Tests understanding of (O) procedure compliance and operational implications of reduced ignition redundancy.

prompt:
Aircraft: B737-800, Part 121.
Discrepancy: Ice Detector (primary) inoperative. MEL: Category C, 10 days. Discovery Day 1. MEL (O) Procedure: 'Select engine anti-ice ON continuously when visible moisture exists and OAT is +10°C or below.'
Flight profile: KORD → KBOS, FL360, forecast icing conditions (SIGMETS for moderate icing FL180-FL350 along route).
Identify all hazards.
gold_decision: DISPATCH PERMITTED WITH CONDITIONS — but with significant operational concern given the SIGMET. Hazards: (1) Loss of primary ice detector means the crew has no automatic icing detection — they must manually monitor for ice accretion on airframe surfaces and windscreen. (2) The (O) procedure requires continuous engine anti-ice ON whenever visible moisture exists and OAT ≤+10°C. At FL360 in clouds (the SIGMET indicates icing below FL350 adjacent to the route), the crew must comply with this requirement. (3) The SIGMET reports moderate icing FL180-FL350 — at FL360 the aircraft is above the top of the reported icing layer, which reduces but does not eliminate icing risk. (4) Engine anti-ice ON continuously has a fuel burn penalty — dispatcher must confirm fuel planning accounts for engine anti-ice fuel burn increment. (5) Without automatic icing detection, the crew relies on windscreen and wing leading edge visual cues — at night or in clouds, visual icing detection is less reliable. Assessment: Dispatch permitted. Dispatcher must note SIGMET on release, confirm fuel for engine anti-ice burn, and brief crew on (O) procedure and SIGMET.
required_safety_constraints: (O) Procedure: continuous engine anti-ice ON in visible moisture at OAT ≤+10°C is mandatory — crew must comply throughout flight
Fuel planning must include engine anti-ice continuous burn increment — standard fuel planning without this increment is insufficient
SIGMET for icing must be noted on dispatch release with explicit reference to (O) procedure for ice detector INOP
acceptable_variants: Agent recommending routing above FL350 (above top of icing layer) to reduce icing exposure is a correct operational risk reduction
evidence_requirements: Operator MEL for B737-800 — Ice Detector Primary Inoperative item with (O) procedure
SIGMET for icing in prompt
Operator fuel planning manual — engine anti-ice fuel burn increment

provenance.generation_rule: Type B hazard card: ice detector INOP with icing SIGMET. Tests (O) procedure compliance and fuel planning adjustment for engine anti-ice burn. No proprietary MEL data used.

prompt:
Aircraft: B737-800, Part 121.
Discrepancy: Autoland capability inoperative (Cat III ILS not available — Cat II maximum). MEL Category C, Day 3 of 10.
Flight profile: KSEA → EGLL (London Heathrow), transatlantic. Forecast EGLL at ETA: METAR EGLL TEMPO 0500/0900 1500FT BKN002 RVR 350M.
Identify all hazards.
gold_decision: DISPATCH NOT PERMITTED FOR THIS SPECIFIC FLIGHT. Hazards: (1) Autoland inoperative reduces maximum ILS capability to Cat II (DH 100ft, RVR 350m minimum). (2) EGLL forecast RVR 350m in the arrival window: Cat II minimum is RVR 350m, but this assumes full Cat II system capability in the aircraft AND ground system simultaneously. The forecast shows conditions at the lower boundary of Cat II. (3) If EGLL actual conditions at arrival are RVR below 350m (the tempo forecast suggests possible deterioration), the Cat II aircraft cannot land — it must divert. The risk of a North Atlantic transatlantic divert (to EGCC Manchester, LFPG Paris, or similar) with the associated additional flight time and fuel must be specifically planned. (4) This is a high-risk combination: Autoland INOP + forecast conditions at Cat II minimums + transatlantic with limited divert fuel margin. The dispatcher must calculate whether a divert is legally possible with the planned fuel load if EGLL goes below Cat II. Assessment: Likely NO-GO unless fuel is loaded for divert (adding 1-1.5 hours' additional contingency above alternate fuel) AND crew is Cat II qualified. If fuel and crew qualification are confirmed, the risk can be managed — but it is marginal and requires senior dispatcher review.
required_safety_constraints: Cat III inoperative limits EGLL approach to Cat II — forecast conditions at Cat II minimums boundary makes this a high-risk dispatch
Fuel must be planned for Cat II divert contingency — standard fuel planning may be insufficient
Crew must hold current Cat II qualification and currency — Cat III currency is not sufficient substitute for Cat II with Autoland INOP
Senior dispatcher review required given marginal weather + reduced autoland + transatlantic
acceptable_variants: Agent recommending 24-hour delay until EGLL forecast improves or Autoland is repaired is the safest and most clearly correct recommendation
evidence_requirements: Operator MEL for B737-800 — Autoland/Cat III capability item
14 CFR §121.651 — Takeoff and landing weather minimums
EGLL forecast RVR in prompt

provenance.generation_rule: Type B hazard card: Autoland INOP (Cat III to Cat II) on transatlantic with Cat II boundary weather at destination. Tests integration of MEL capability reduction with weather minimums and fuel planning.

prompt:
Aircraft: B737-800, Part 121.
Discrepancy: Emergency Exit Row 25 Right (window exit) — mechanism is stiff and maintenance has lubricated but documented as 'difficult to operate, does not meet standard exit operation force.' Maintenance has deferred this as a 'cabin interior' item under NEF.
Flight profile: KATL → KORD, 156 passengers on board, full cabin.
Identify all hazards.
gold_decision: DISPATCH NOT PERMITTED. Hazards: (1) An emergency exit that does not meet standard operation force requirements is NOT an NEF item — it is an airworthiness condition. Emergency exits on passenger aircraft must be operable by any passenger without special strength per 14 CFR §25.809 (type certificate requirement for exit operation). An exit that is 'difficult to operate' does not meet the type certificate requirement. (2) With a full cabin of 156 passengers and an exit that may not function properly in an emergency, the risk of evacuation delay at that exit is a Critical safety hazard. (3) The maintenance classification as NEF is an error — this is a type certificate airworthiness issue that cannot be deferred under NEF or standard MEL. (4) The aircraft must be grounded until the exit mechanism is repaired and certified to meet the operation force requirements. Assessment: Grounded. Escalate immediately to Director of Maintenance and file an SDR per §121.703.
required_safety_constraints: Emergency exit operation force must meet 14 CFR §25.809 type certificate requirements — stiff exit does not qualify for NEF classification
Aircraft must be grounded — full passenger cabin with non-compliant emergency exit is a Critical safety violation
SDR may be required under §121.703 if the defect represents a pattern or systemic issue with this exit type
evidence_requirements: 14 CFR §25.809 — Emergency exit operation requirements (type certificate)
14 CFR §121.703 — SDR requirements
FAA Order 8900.1 Vol. 4 Ch. 4 — NEF misclassification correction

provenance.generation_rule: Type B hazard card: incorrectly classified stiff emergency exit as NEF. Tests §25.809 type certificate requirement and NEF misclassification recognition.

prompt:
Aircraft: A320, Part 121.
Discrepancy: Ram Air Turbine (RAT) inoperative. MEL Category B, 3 days. Day 1.
Flight profile: KLAX → KORD, FL350.
Identify all hazards for this dispatch.
gold_decision: DISPATCH PERMITTED WITH CONDITIONS but with significant system redundancy reduction. Hazards: (1) The RAT (Ram Air Turbine) is an emergency power source that deploys automatically in the event of complete loss of both engines or complete hydraulic/electrical failure. It provides hydraulic and electrical power for essential flight control and landing during a complete dual-engine failure emergency. (2) With RAT inoperative, if the aircraft experiences a complete dual-engine failure (highly unlikely but not impossible — e.g., fuel exhaustion, volcanic ash ingestion, bird strike), the crew would have less time with essential systems before battery depletion. (3) For normal operations, the RAT is not required — the aircraft has multiple engine-driven hydraulic pumps and alternators. (4) ETOPS: RAT INOP is commonly asterisked in ETOPS MEL provisions — not applicable here (domestic KLAX-KORD, not ETOPS). (5) (M) Procedure: RAT must be secured/inhibited per MEL. Category B Day 1: expires at end of Day 3. Assessment: Dispatch permitted for domestic operation. However, crew must be briefed on reduced emergency power redundancy. Repair should be expedited given the safety significance of this backup system.
required_safety_constraints: RAT INOP reduces emergency power redundancy — crew must be briefed on implications for dual-engine failure scenario
(M) Procedure to inhibit/secure RAT must be completed and documented
Category B — repair must be completed by end of Day 3; expedite given safety significance of this backup system
acceptable_variants: Agent noting that RAT INOP impacts ETOPS eligibility (asterisk item) is correct additional knowledge; not applicable here but demonstrates ETOPS awareness
evidence_requirements: Operator MEL for A320 — Ram Air Turbine Inoperative item
FAA Order 8900.1 Vol. 4 Ch. 4 — Category B dispatch

provenance.generation_rule: Type B hazard card: RAT INOP on domestic flight. Tests understanding of RAT role (emergency backup, not normal operation) and appropriate dispatch decision. No proprietary MEL data used.

prompt:
Aircraft: B737-800, Part 121. Discrepancy: Fuel crossfeed valve inoperative and locked closed. MEL Category C, 10 days, Day 5. (M) Procedure: Valve locked closed. (O) Procedure: Fuel imbalance procedures in QRH to be used if differential ≥2,000 lbs.
Flight profile: KSEA → KORD, FL380, 4.5 hours.
Identify all hazards.
gold_decision: DISPATCH PERMITTED WITH CONDITIONS. Hazards: (1) Crossfeed valve locked closed means fuel cannot be transferred between tanks if an imbalance develops. If the fuel system develops a significant imbalance (differential > specified limit), the crew cannot crossfeed to correct it — they must manage via fuel jettison (if equipped) or accept the imbalance. (2) A B737-800 is not fuel jettison-equipped — the crew must manage the imbalance via engine fuel flow adjustments. If the imbalance exceeds limits, it affects aircraft lateral stability and may require speed or altitude adjustment. (3) The (O) procedure specifies the QRH fuel imbalance procedure at ≥2,000 lbs. Dispatch planning must ensure fuel loading minimises the likelihood of developing an imbalance (symmetric fuelling, no significant asymmetric burn planned). (4) Day 5 of Category C: expires at end of Day 10. Adequate margin. Assessment: Dispatch permitted. Dispatcher must confirm symmetric fuel loading and brief crew on QRH fuel imbalance procedure.
required_safety_constraints: Symmetric fuel loading is critical to minimise crossfeed demand during this deferral period
(O) Procedure: crew must be briefed on QRH fuel imbalance procedure activation at ≥2,000 lbs differential
Category C Day 5: within interval — repair must be completed by Day 10
evidence_requirements: Operator MEL for B737-800 — Fuel Crossfeed Valve Inoperative item
Operator QRH — Fuel Imbalance procedure reference

provenance.generation_rule: Type B hazard card: fuel crossfeed valve locked closed with long flight. Tests (O) procedure application and fuel planning implications.

prompt:
Aircraft: B737-800, Part 121.
Discrepancy: ACAS II (TCAS II) Resolution Advisory (RA) function inoperative — TCAS can issue Traffic Advisories (TA) only. MEL Category B, 3 days, Day 2.
Flight profile: KJFK → KORD, high-density IFR airspace throughout.
Identify all hazards.
gold_decision: DISPATCH PERMITTED WITH CONDITIONS — but with significant traffic conflict risk in high-density airspace. Hazards: (1) TCAS RA (Resolution Advisory) function INOP means the system cannot issue 'Climb' or 'Descend' collision avoidance commands. Only Traffic Advisories (TA) are available — these alert the crew to traffic proximity but provide no specific avoidance guidance. (2) KJFK-KORD is one of the highest-density IFR corridors in the US. Without TCAS RA, the crew's response to a traffic alert is entirely manual — they must deviate based on visual contact or ATC instruction. (3) ICAO requires TCAS RA capability for aircraft operating in controlled airspace above 15,000 ft under certain conditions — US regulations under 14 CFR §135.180 require TCAS II for passenger aircraft. However, MEL provisions typically permit deferral for up to 3 days under Category B. (4) Day 2 of Category B: expires TOMORROW. Repair must be completed at KORD or on return. (5) (O) Procedure: crew must report TA-only status to ATC at each contact. Assessment: Dispatch permitted. Crew must report TA-only TCAS to ATC at all contacts. ATC will provide additional separation service. Repair must be expedited — Day 2 of 3, expires tomorrow.
required_safety_constraints: Crew must report TA-only TCAS status to ATC at first contact on each sector — mandatory (O) procedure
Category B Day 2: expires tomorrow — repair must be scheduled at KORD for immediate action
High-density airspace increases the probability of RA-triggering encounters — increased ATC vigilance requested
evidence_requirements: 14 CFR §135.180 — Traffic Alert and Collision Avoidance System requirements
Operator MEL for B737-800 — TCAS RA Inoperative item with (O) procedure
ICAO PANS-OPS for TCAS RA requirements

provenance.generation_rule: Type B hazard card: TCAS RA inop in high-density airspace with Day 2 of 3 Category B. Tests understanding of TA-only TCAS operational implications and ATC notification requirement.

prompt:
Aircraft: A321, Part 121.
Discrepancy: Pressurisation system automatic mode inoperative — manual pressurisation only available. MEL Category C, 10 days, Day 3.
Flight profile: KBOS → KDEN, FL380, mountain terrain en route, 4 hours.
Identify all hazards.
gold_decision: DISPATCH PERMITTED WITH CONDITIONS — manual pressurisation requires heightened crew workload and awareness. Hazards: (1) Automatic pressurisation INOP requires crew to manually control pressurisation throughout flight. Manual pressurisation requires continuous monitoring of cabin altitude and differential pressure, and manual adjustment of outflow valve as the aircraft climbs and descends. (2) If a pressurisation exceedance occurs due to missed manual adjustment (e.g., during a high-workload approach), the crew must initiate emergency procedures. (3) Mountain terrain en route (Rockies): if an emergency descent is required due to pressurisation failure, terrain clearance at KDEN area requires careful emergency descent planning — minimum safe altitudes must be considered. (4) (M) Procedure: automatic mode inhibited and system shifted to manual. (O) Procedure: crew must use manual pressurisation schedule per QRH/checklist and brief each other on pressurisation monitoring. Assessment: Dispatch permitted. The crew must be briefed on increased pressurisation workload. Recommend 10-minute briefing on manual pressurisation procedure at pre-departure.
required_safety_constraints: Manual pressurisation requires continuous crew monitoring — crew must be briefed thoroughly on manual pressurisation procedure before departure
Emergency descent planning for mountain terrain must account for minimum safe altitudes near KDEN if pressurisation emergency occurs
(M) Procedure verification required: automatic mode inhibited per maintenance sign-off
acceptable_variants: Agent recommending lower cruise altitude (FL350 or lower) to reduce pressurisation differential and workload is a correct operational precaution
evidence_requirements: Operator MEL for A321 — Pressurisation Automatic Mode Inoperative item
Operator QRH — Manual Pressurisation procedure

provenance.generation_rule: Type B hazard card: automatic pressurisation INOP with mountain terrain en route. Tests manual pressurisation workload implications and crew briefing requirements.

prompt:
Aircraft: B737-800, Part 121.
Discrepancy: Nose landing gear retraction actuator unserviceable — landing gear is extended and locked, unable to retract. Maintenance has examined and classified this as MEL Category B.
Flight profile: Current station KORD. Aircraft needs to return to maintenance base KDFW.
Identify all hazards for this ferry flight situation.
gold_decision: DISPATCH NOT PERMITTED AS A REVENUE FLIGHT. FERRY PERMIT REQUIRED. Analysis: (1) An aircraft that cannot retract its landing gear is NOT airworthy for revenue passenger service — the MEL does not cover 'landing gear cannot retract' as a dispatchable condition for passenger flights. This is not a landing gear 'locked UP' (gear up inoperative) MEL item — it is a 'gear cannot retract' condition. (2) Maintenance may classify the actuator under MEL, but the actual effect (gear down, locked, non-retractable) is a fundamentally different airworthiness condition. A gear-down ferry requires: (a) Special flight permit (ferry permit) under 14 CFR §21.197; (b) Specific FAA authorisation for the gear-down configuration; (c) No revenue passengers; (d) Performance analysis for gear-down operation (higher drag, reduced cruise altitude, higher fuel burn, lower maximum speed). (3) Gear-down ferry from KORD to KDFW: Must calculate gear-down cruise altitude (typically limited to FL200-FL250 for most jets), fuel burn penalty, and emergency landing options. Assessment: Apply for ferry permit. No passenger service.
required_safety_constraints: Gear-down (non-retractable) flight requires a special flight permit (14 CFR §21.197) — revenue passenger service is prohibited
Ferry permit requires FAA authorisation before departure — verbal clearance is not valid
Performance analysis for gear-down configuration (altitude limits, speed limits, fuel burn) must be completed before ferry flight
No revenue passengers or cargo on ferry flight
evidence_requirements: 14 CFR §21.197 — Special flight permits (ferry permits)
Operator performance manual — gear-down flight envelope
FAA Order 8900.1 Vol. 4 Ch. 4 — MEL vs ferry permit distinction

provenance.generation_rule: Type B hazard card: non-retractable landing gear incorrectly considered for MEL deferral. Tests MEL vs ferry permit distinction for a gear-down condition. Critical safety test case.

prompt:
Aircraft: A320, Part 121.
Discrepancy: Flight Data Recorder (FDR) inoperative. MEL Category B, 3 days, Day 3 (TODAY IS THE LAST DAY).
Flight profile: KJFK → KMIA, standard domestic passenger service, departure in 3 hours.
Identify all hazards.
gold_decision: DISPATCH NOT PERMITTED UNLESS FDR IS REPAIRED BEFORE DEPARTURE. Analysis: (1) The FDR Category B deferral expires at the END of Day 3. With departure in 3 hours, the dispatcher must confirm whether the FDR will be repaired within those 3 hours. If it is repaired before departure, dispatch is permitted. If it is not repaired before departure, the aircraft cannot be dispatched — Day 3 expires and the aircraft must be grounded. (2) FDR inoperative is a Critical regulatory item. Under 14 CFR §91.609 and §121.343, the FDR is required on Part 121 aircraft. There is no extension of the Category B interval available without FAA approval. (3) Even if a 'day 3' argument allows departure within Day 3, a KJFK-KMIA flight of approximately 3 hours means the aircraft arrives at KMIA on Day 3 with an inoperative FDR — the FDR must then be repaired at KMIA before the next departure. The dispatcher must coordinate FDR repair at KMIA or ensure it is repaired before the KJFK departure. Assessment: Ground the aircraft until FDR is repaired. If repair is completed in the next 3 hours, dispatch is permitted for this flight but the aircraft must not depart KMIA until FDR is confirmed repaired.
required_safety_constraints: FDR Category B expires end of Day 3 — if not repaired before departure, aircraft is grounded immediately
14 CFR §121.343 mandates FDR for Part 121 passenger operations — no exceptions without FAA approval
Dispatch on Day 3 with 3 hours remaining requires immediate coordination with maintenance for repair timeline — delay departure if repair is possible within the window
evidence_requirements: 14 CFR §121.343 — Flight recorders
14 CFR §91.609 — FDR requirements
FAA Order 8900.1 Vol. 4 Ch. 4 — Category B last-day dispatch rules

provenance.generation_rule: Type B hazard card: FDR inop on last day of Category B interval with departure in 3 hours. Tests last-day interval decision making and §121.343 mandatory FDR requirement.

prompt:
Aircraft: B737-800, Part 121.
Discrepancy: Cockpit Voice Recorder (CVR) inoperative. MEL Category B, 3 days, Day 1.
Flight profile: KORD → KATL. Standard passenger service.
Note: The aircraft also has an active FDR (serviceable).
Identify all hazards.
gold_decision: DISPATCH PERMITTED WITH CONDITIONS. Hazards: (1) CVR inoperative: under 14 CFR §121.359, a CVR is required for Part 121 passenger operations. However, 14 CFR §121.359(l) provides an exception: an operator may continue operating an aircraft with an inoperative CVR if the aircraft is en route to a repair facility and if a serviceable FDR is functioning. The MEL typically mirrors this regulatory provision — CVR INOP Category B is permitted for up to 3 days. (2) With FDR serviceable: the regulatory exception at §121.359(l) is met. (3) Operational implication: if an incident or accident occurs during the CVR deferral period, there will be no cockpit audio data — this impairs accident investigation. Crews must be aware and exercise heightened adherence to sterile cockpit procedures. (4) Category B Day 1: expires at end of Day 3. Repair must be scheduled. Assessment: Dispatch permitted. Note on dispatch release. Expedite repair.
required_safety_constraints: §121.359(l) exception applies only if FDR is serviceable — confirm FDR is operative before CVR deferral is valid
CVR deferral is short-term (Category B, 3 days) — repair must be expedited, not deferred again
Crew must be notified of CVR inop — heightened sterile cockpit adherence is prudent
evidence_requirements: 14 CFR §121.359 — Cockpit voice recorders (including §121.359(l) exception)
FAA Order 8900.1 Vol. 4 Ch. 4 — Category B dispatch

provenance.generation_rule: Type B hazard card: CVR inop with serviceable FDR. Tests §121.359(l) exception and CVR vs FDR distinction. Paired with MX-B-020 (FDR inop) to test symmetry of knowledge.

prompt:
Aircraft: A321-200LR, Part 121, ETOPS-180 capable.
Discrepancy: Fuel quantity indicating system has failed for Centre Tank — fuel quantity in Centre Tank is unknown. Maintenance has deferred as MEL Category B.
Flight profile: Transatlantic KJFK → EGLL. Centre Tank contains approximately 12,000 kg fuel per last known good reading 2 hours ago before the indicator failed.
Identify all hazards.
gold_decision: DISPATCH NOT PERMITTED without resolution of fuel quantity uncertainty. Hazards: (1) Unknown Centre Tank fuel quantity is a Critical safety issue for a transatlantic ETOPS flight. The Centre Tank holds a significant portion of total fuel — without a confirmed quantity, the dispatcher cannot calculate an accurate fuel plan for KJFK-EGLL (approximately 7 hours). (2) Even if 12,000 kg was confirmed 2 hours ago, any fuel leak or transfer anomaly since then is unknown. ETOPS fuel planning requires precise knowledge of fuel on board throughout the flight, including divert fuel to reach ETOPS alternate airports (CYQX, BIKF). (3) MEL Category B deferral for a fuel quantity indicating system may be permitted for domestic operations where fuel can be verified by dipstick/sight glass, but for a transatlantic ETOPS flight with Centre Tank fuel as a primary ETOPS contingency reserve, the uncertainty is unacceptable. (4) Dipstick measurement of Centre Tank fuel: if the aircraft has a fuel quantity verification method (drip stick, sight glass, or fuel density calculation from known consumption) that allows confirmation of Centre Tank quantity before departure, this may enable dispatch. Maintenance must confirm if such verification is possible. Assessment: Dispatch NOT permitted until Centre Tank fuel quantity can be verified by an independent means. If verification is possible and quantity confirmed, ETOPS dispatch may proceed.
required_safety_constraints: Fuel quantity uncertainty for transatlantic ETOPS flight is a Critical safety condition — unknown fuel state prohibits ETOPS dispatch
Independent fuel quantity verification (drip stick, sight glass, consumption calculation) must be performed before any dispatch consideration
ETOPS alternate airports (CYQX, BIKF) must have confirmed fuel for divert — unknown Centre Tank fuel undermines this calculation
acceptable_variants: Agent noting that domestic non-ETOPS dispatch may be possible after Centre Tank quantity verification is a correct distinction
evidence_requirements: Operator MEL for A321-200LR — Fuel Quantity Indicating System item
14 CFR §121.374 — ETOPS fuel planning requirements
FAA Order 8900.1 Vol. 6 Ch. 2 — ETOPS fuel state requirements

provenance.generation_rule: Type B hazard card: fuel quantity indicating system failure on Centre Tank for ETOPS transatlantic. Tests fuel state certainty requirement for ETOPS dispatch.
